structured_choices.data_source: Historical shipping route dataset spanning 36 months from a single logistics operator. Dataset includes ~1,000–2,000 daily routes with complete records: route ID, origin, destination, customer location, scheduled departure/arrival, actual arrival time, traffic conditions at time of route execution, weather conditions, vehicle type, driver ID, and delay cause classification. Data quality: checked for missing values in critical fields (actual arrival time, scheduled time); records with >5% missing features excluded; temporal consistency verified (no retroactive modifications to historical timestamps).
structured_choices.label_definition: On-time delivery binary label: delivery arrives within ±15 minutes of scheduled arrival time = 1 (on-time); arrival >15 minutes late = 0 (delayed). The 15-minute threshold reflects operational SLA standards. Secondary outcome: continuous delay magnitude in minutes (for sensitivity analysis). Label is deterministic from arrival records; no manual labeling required.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split across three windows to prevent information leakage and reflect real-world deployment: Training set: months 1–18 (50% of data). Validation set: months 19–27 (33% of data). Test set: months 28–36 (17% of data, held-out until final evaluation). Justification: RL agents must learn patterns from historical data without seeing future routes or conditions. Temporal splits prevent the agent from 'seeing' routing decisions and outcomes it should learn to predict. This also simulates realistic deployment: model trained on past, evaluated on unseen future conditions. No stratification by route complexity or weather needed within each window; stratification by month ensures seasonal variations are present in all splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers for traffic/weather features, categorical encoders for vehicle type/driver ID) are fit on the training set only (months 1–18) and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage from validation/test statistics into the training process. Discrete features (weather category, vehicle type) are one-hot encoded; continuous features (travel distance, historical traffic severity) are standardized using z-score scaling fit on training set. Any imputation strategy (e.g., forward-fill for missing traffic data) is applied using only training set statistics.
structured_choices.features: State representation for RL agent: (1) Route features: origin–destination pair, distance, time-of-day, day-of-week, seasonal indicator; (2) Traffic/weather context: real-time traffic level (categorical: low/medium/high), weather conditions (categorical: clear/rain/snow), historical delay rate on this route in the past 7 days; (3) Vehicle/operational: vehicle capacity utilization, driver experience level (tenure bucketed), vehicle type. All features are lagged appropriately: only information available *at route planning time* is included; no post-hoc delivery outcome data is used as a feature.
structured_choices.model: Reinforcement learning agent using Deep Q-Network (DQN) or actor-critic architecture (A3C). The agent learns a policy π that selects routing decisions (route segment sequence, timing) to maximize cumulative on-time delivery over an episode (single route or daily route batch). The action space is discrete: selection from candidate route options generated by a baseline routing engine. The environment is simulated using the historical data: given a state (route context), the agent selects a route action, and the outcome (on-time/delayed) is determined by the historical record for that action under similar conditions. Training uses standard RL techniques: experience replay, target network, ε-greedy exploration (annealed from ε=1.0 to ε=0.05 over training).
structured_choices.baseline: Static optimization baseline: a deterministic routing algorithm (e.g., nearest-neighbor with real-time traffic adjustment, or a hand-tuned heuristic used in current operations). This baseline is given equivalent effort: hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set using grid search or Bayesian optimization to maximize on-time delivery rate, identical to RL tuning. The baseline uses the same feature set and is evaluated under identical conditions (same test set, same metrics, same stratification checks). This ensures the hypothesis tests whether adaptive exploration strategies add value over well-tuned static methods, not whether an RL model beats a naive baseline.
structured_choices.primary_metric: On-time delivery rate (%) on the test set: (number of on-time deliveries / total deliveries) × 100. This directly aligns with stakeholder objective (customer satisfaction via timely delivery) and operational KPI. Success threshold: RL agent achieves ≥25% relative improvement over baseline on-time rate. Example: if baseline achieves 85% on-time, RL must achieve ≥91.25%. Justification: this metric is interpretable to stakeholders, causally linked to customer satisfaction, and robust to class imbalance (if ~80% of routes are on-time historically, the metric still meaningfully captures RL improvement).
secondary_metrics: Mean delay magnitude (minutes): average delay for delayed deliveries only. Captures severity of failures.
Delay distribution percentiles (p50, p75, p90): identifies whether RL reduces tail risk (worst-case delays).
Stratified on-time rate by route complexity (short/medium/long distance) and weather condition: checks whether RL generalizes across diverse conditions or overfit to specific scenario types.
Computational cost / decision latency: ensures RL agent's route selection does not introduce unacceptable delays in operational deployment.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both RL agent and baseline are tuned using the validation set (months 19–27) only. Model selection metric: on-time delivery rate on validation set. Perform random search or grid search over RL hyperparameters (learning rate, exploration schedule, network architecture) and baseline parameters (route heuristic weights, traffic adjustment factors). The model with highest validation on-time rate is then evaluated once on the test set. Validation set is used iteratively (multiple hyperparameter trials); test set is untouched during tuning.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 28–36) is used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, after all model selection and tuning are complete. No metric from the test set informs any tuning or design decision. This is a strict hold-out evaluation, ensuring the test set provides an honest estimate of how the RL agent would perform in real deployment on unseen future data.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: external factors (weather, traffic regulations, infrastructure changes) that vary over time and affect delivery times independently of the routing algorithm. Controls: (1) Stratify test set analysis by weather condition and traffic level to measure RL performance within each condition separately, isolating algorithm improvement from environmental variation. (2) Include weather and traffic features as inputs to both RL and baseline models, so both have access to the same contextual information; the comparison isolates the *algorithmic choice* (static vs. adaptive exploration), not information availability. (3) Check for distribution shift: verify that the distribution of route types, traffic conditions, and weather in the test set is similar to training/validation. Large shifts indicate confounding due to seasonal or operational changes; flag these in the report. (4) Perform causal sensitivity analysis: re-evaluate baseline and RL agent on test set sub-cohorts (e.g., routes executed during the same traffic regime) to confirm relative gains are not artifacts of cohort differences. Scope: this experiment establishes whether RL with adaptive exploration outperforms static optimization *under the conditions present in the test set*. It does not establish causality in the presence of unmeasured confounds (e.g., driver behavior changes, fleet maintenance schedules) that may correlate with time period. Results are specific to this operator's data; generalization to other operators or geographies requires separate evaluation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Adaptive RL for Logistics Routing

### 1. Data and Label Definition

This experiment uses 36 months of historical shipping records from a single logistics operator, spanning approximately 1,000–2,000 daily routes. The dataset includes timestamped route information (origin, destination, customer location), scheduled and actual arrival times, real-time traffic and weather conditions, vehicle metadata, and driver information.

The outcome label is a binary on-time delivery indicator: a route is marked on-time if it arrives within ±15 minutes of the scheduled arrival time (reflecting operational SLA), and delayed otherwise. This is a deterministic label derived directly from historical records; no manual annotation is required. A secondary continuous outcome (delay magnitude in minutes) will be used for sensitivity analysis.

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split Strategy

A temporal split is applied to prevent information leakage and reflect real-world deployment:
- **Training set:** Months 1–18 (50% of data)
- **Validation set:** Months 19–27 (33% of data)
- **Test set:** Months 28–36 (17% of data, held-out until final evaluation)

Temporal splits are essential for reinforcement learning: the agent must learn from historical routing decisions and outcomes without access to future information or decisions. This structure also captures seasonal variations and traffic pattern shifts across the dataset, ensuring the model learns robust policies. No stratification by route type is performed within each window; stratification by month ensures representativeness of all operational conditions across splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The RL agent's state representation includes three categories of features:
- **Route context:** origin–destination pair, distance, time-of-day, day-of-week, seasonal indicator
- **Real-time conditions:** traffic level (categorical: low/medium/high), weather (categorical: clear/rain/snow), historical delay rate on this route in the past 7 days
- **Vehicle and operations:** vehicle utilization, driver experience level (tenure buckets), vehicle type

All features reflect information available *at route planning time*; no post-hoc delivery outcomes are used as features. Categorical variables are one-hot encoded, and continuous variables are standardized using z-score scaling. All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation rules) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets to prevent data leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**RL Model:** A Deep Q-Network (DQN) or actor-critic agent (e.g., A3C) that learns a policy to select routing decisions (route segment sequences, timing) to maximize on-time delivery. The action space is discrete: the agent selects from candidate routes generated by a baseline routing engine. The environment is simulated: given the state, the agent takes an action, and the outcome is determined by the historical record for similar conditions. Training employs standard RL techniques: experience replay, target networks, and ε-greedy exploration (annealed from ε=1.0 to ε=0.05).

**Baseline:** A deterministic static routing algorithm (e.g., nearest-neighbor with real-time traffic adjustment, or the company's current hand-tuned heuristic). Critically, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters are optimized on the validation set using grid search or Bayesian optimization to maximize on-time delivery rate, using the same feature set and evaluation conditions as the RL agent. This ensures the hypothesis tests whether *adaptive exploration* provides an advantage over well-tuned static methods, not whether RL beats a naive approach.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** On-time delivery rate (%) on the test set, calculated as (on-time deliveries / total deliveries) × 100. This metric directly aligns with stakeholder objectives (customer satisfaction and timely delivery) and is an operational KPI. Success is defined as a ≥25% relative improvement: if the baseline achieves 85% on-time, the RL agent must achieve ≥91.25%.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Mean and percentile delay magnitudes (p50, p75, p90) for delayed deliveries, capturing failure severity and tail risk.
- Stratified on-time rates by route complexity and weather condition, to detect whether RL generalizes across diverse scenarios or overfits.
- Computational latency of routing decisions, ensuring deployment feasibility.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both RL and baseline models are tuned using the validation set (months 19–27) only. Model selection employs grid or random search over RL hyperparameters (learning rate, exploration schedule, network depth) and baseline parameters (heuristic weights, traffic adjustment factors). The model maximizing on-time delivery rate on the validation set is selected. The validation set is used iteratively across tuning trials; the test set remains untouched during tuning and is evaluated exactly once at the end.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

External factors—weather, traffic patterns, regulatory changes—affect delivery times independently of the routing algorithm. To isolate algorithmic improvements, the design includes:
- **Feature parity:** Both RL and baseline receive weather and traffic features as inputs, so both models have access to the same contextual information.
- **Stratified analysis:** Test set results are broken down by weather condition and traffic level, allowing measurement of RL performance within each condition separately.
- **Distribution shift detection:** Verify that the distribution of route types, traffic, and weather in the test set matches training/validation; large shifts are flagged as confounding from operational changes.
- **Causal sensitivity:** Re-evaluate both models on test sub-cohorts defined by traffic regime to confirm gains are not artifacts of cohort differences.

This experiment establishes whether RL with adaptive exploration outperforms static optimization under the conditions present in the test set. It does not establish causality in the presence of unmeasured confounds (e.g., driver behavior, maintenance schedules). Results generalize to this operator's data; evaluation on other operators or regions requires a separate study.
